text: At the beginning of the procedure, the robotic system is docked with instruments in place. The gallbladder is visible, and the liver appears brown. The liver briefly turns green, demonstrating the use of ICG and Firefly mode.

The surgeon begins the robotic cholecystectomy using a dome down technique, starting at the top of the gallbladder. A small window is created to lift the gallbladder, facilitating the dissection process.

The video shows the gallbladder, affected by acute cholecystitis, with visible edema and fluid accumulation between the liver and gallbladder. The gallbladder appears closely attached to the liver, and the dissection is performed at increased speed. The use of ICG dye and Firefly mode is briefly mentioned.

The procedure continues under regular lighting, with slight bleeding visible. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand for dissection, while the left hand operates a fenestrated bipolar instrument for electrocautery. A third instrument retracts the liver upwards, gradually elevating it to improve exposure as dissection progresses.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver, revealing scarred tissue indicative of acute on chronic cholecystitis.
The surgical field is viewed in Firefly mode, where the liver appears bright green due to the ICG dye, while the gallbladder remains gray, indicating a lack of dye uptake due to acute cholecystitis.

In Firefly mode, the surgeon continues to dissect the gallbladder from the liver, approaching the neck of the gallbladder where the ducts are located. The bright green visualization helps delineate the interface between the gallbladder and liver tissue.

The surgeon uses bipolar electrocautery to achieve hemostasis, controlling minor bleeding. The gallbladder is further freed from the liver, which is retracted upwards. Yellow fatty tissue is visible, and the green common bile duct is seen extending vertically.

In the robotic cholecystectomy procedure, the surgeon uses a specialized imaging mode to visualize the anatomy at the neck of the gallbladder. The gallbladder shows minimal green fluorescence due to limited dye uptake, indicating the presence of the cystic duct. The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder, identifying safe areas to divide while avoiding critical structures.

The surgeon continues to free the gallbladder, revealing more of the anatomical structures. The video shows a clear Y configuration with dye visible within the gallbladder. To the right of the instrument, the cystic duct is seen joining the common bile duct, providing a clear view of the anatomy.

The surgeon switches between regular and Firefly modes, highlighting the cystic duct, which is being freed from surrounding tissue. The gallbladder is visible to the left, with fatty tissue being dissected away. The cystic duct is clearly seen, with the common bile duct on the right, extending upwards into the liver and downwards towards the intestines.

The surgeon observes green bile in the duodenum and antrum, indicating bile flow into the intestines. Using a clip applier, two permanent clips are placed on the cystic duct. The same instrument is then used to divide the cystic duct, completing this part of the procedure.
